_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 7-20 Spinal Precautions (Revised 5/26/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Procedures Protocol Section 7-20: Spinal Precautions — MDHHS Approved 5/26/2023
purpose: Govern prehospital spinal precautions. Positive spinal assessment → precautions. Penetrating trauma requires precautions only if focal neurological deficit is present. Airway takes priority over spinal stability. Rigid immobilization is avoided in combative patients.

sections.title: Indications for Spinal Precautions
points: Perform spinal assessment on all patients with a mechanism of injury that could cause spinal injury
POSITIVE SPINAL ASSESSMENT: apply spinal precautions when assessment is positive
PENETRATING TRAUMA: spinal precautions are indicated ONLY if a focal neurological deficit is present (do not apply cervical collar or immobilize in penetrating trauma without neuro deficit)
PATIENTS ≥ 65 YEARS with a head strike mechanism: apply c-collar even if the spinal assessment is NEGATIVE

sections.title: Spinal Precaution Techniques
points: Apply cervical collar in neutral alignment
Do NOT tape the patient's head directly to the cot/board — support the head with padding and straps while maintaining neutral alignment
Maintain manual inline spinal stabilization during patient movement and transfers
Ambulatory patient with positive spinal assessment: apply c-collar and place directly on stretcher/cot — avoid unnecessary transfers to long backboard
Major trauma requiring extrication: long backboard may be used DURING extrication for mechanical advantage; reassess need after extrication

sections.title: Rapid Extrication
points: Rapid extrication (expedited movement without full packaging) is indicated when the patient has:
— Airway or breathing compromise requiring immediate intervention
— Hemodynamic shock
— Unconsciousness or rapidly deteriorating mental status
Maintain as much spinal stabilization as practical during rapid extrication
Document reason for rapid extrication in PCR

sections.title: Special Circumstances
points: PREGNANT PATIENTS: apply spinal precautions with uterine displacement to the left (tilt board or manually displace uterus) to prevent aortocaval compression
COMBATIVE PATIENT: rigid immobilization (long backboard, c-collar) should be avoided if patient movement against restraints could cause more injury — use clinical judgment; contact Medical Control
AIRWAY: airway management ALWAYS takes priority over spinal stability — manage the airway first
PEDIATRIC CAR SEATS:
— Rear-facing car seat: leave child in seat if stable and seat can be secured in the vehicle
— Car seat with high back (convertible/booster with back): immobilize child in seat, then remove seat from vehicle; immobilize to long board
— Booster seat without back: treat as standard spinal precaution; remove from seat

deterioration_flags: Airway compromise with spinal precautions — manage airway FIRST; spinal stability is secondary to airway
Combative patient in spinal immobilization — avoid rigid immobilization if movement against restraints worsens risk; contact MC
Penetrating trauma — spinal precautions ONLY if focal neurological deficit present; do not apply c-collar without neuro deficit in penetrating mechanism
scope_notes: Penetrating trauma: precautions only if focal neuro deficit
Age ≥ 65 with head strike: c-collar even if negative assessment
Do NOT tape head directly to cot
Rapid extrication: airway/breathing compromise, shock, unconsciousness
Pregnant: uterine displacement to left during immobilization
Airway ALWAYS takes priority over spinal stability
